items.mistake: Adding matrices of incompatible shapes as if entrywise addition always worked.
items.fix: Check that both matrices have exactly the same number of rows and columns before adding or subtracting.
items.why: Matrix addition is defined only entrywise, so each entry in one matrix must have a matching entry in the other. Otherwise the operation is undefined in algebra and should raise an error in code.
items.example: Trying to compute A (2 x 3) + B (3 x 2) is invalid, because A has 2 rows and 3 columns while B has 3 rows and 2 columns.

items.mistake: Forgetting that the transpose changes shape and orientation.
items.fix: Always track the shape before and after transposing, especially when you intend to multiply matrices or stack them.
items.why: If you assume A and A^T have the same shape, you can silently feed wrong shapes into later computations, causing either runtime errors or logically incorrect results.
items.example: If X is 64 x 128 (64 examples, 128 features) and you accidentally treat X^T as another batch, you might feed a 128 x 64 matrix into code expecting rows to be examples.

items.mistake: Confusing matrix addition with matrix multiplication in NumPy code.
items.fix: Use `+` only for entrywise addition and reserve the matrix multiplication operator (e.g. `@` in Python or dedicated APIs) for linear transformations.
items.why: Matrix multiplication has different shape rules and meaning from entrywise addition. Using the wrong operation can produce numbers of the right shape but entirely wrong semantics.
items.example: Computing `pred = X + W` instead of `pred = X @ W` adds weights entrywise instead of applying the linear map represented by W.

items.mistake: Assuming every square matrix has an inverse like the identity does.
items.fix: Remember that only non-singular square matrices have inverses; a matrix with dependent rows or columns is singular and not invertible.
items.why: Attempting to use a non-invertible matrix as if it had an inverse leads to undefined operations in theory and numerical failures in practice.
items.example: The matrix [[1, 2], [2, 4]] is square but singular because the second row is a multiple of the first, so there is no matrix A^-1 such that A A^-1 = I.
